title: AR&D: A Framework for Retrieving and Describing Concepts for Interpreting AudioLLMs
comment: Accepted at International Conference on Acoustics, Speech, and Signal Processing (ICASSP), 2026
summary: Despite strong performance in audio perception tasks, large audio-language models (AudioLLMs) remain opaque to interpretation. A major factor behind this lack of interpretability is that individual neurons in these models frequently activate in response to several unrelated concepts. We introduce the first mechanistic interpretability framework for AudioLLMs, leveraging sparse autoencoders (SAEs) to disentangle polysemantic activations into monosemantic features. Our pipeline identifies representative audio clips, assigns meaningful names via automated captioning, and validates concepts through human evaluation and steering. Experiments show that AudioLLMs encode structured and interpretable features, enhancing transparency and control. This work provides a foundation for trustworthy deployment in high-stakes domains and enables future extensions to larger models, multilingual audio, and more fine-grained paralinguistic features. Project URL: https://townim-faisal.github.io/AutoInterpret-AudioLLM/

AI.tldr: First mechanistic interpretability framework for AudioLLMs using sparse autoencoders to disentangle polysemantic neurons into monosemantic features, enabling better understanding and control of audio-language models.
AI.motivation: Large audio-language models (AudioLLMs) perform well but remain opaque due to polysemantic neurons that activate to multiple unrelated concepts, limiting interpretability and trustworthiness for high-stakes applications.
AI.method: Introduces a pipeline using sparse autoencoders (SAEs) to disentangle polysemantic activations into monosemantic features, identifies representative audio clips, assigns meaningful names via automated captioning, and validates concepts through human evaluation and steering experiments.
AI.result: Experiments demonstrate that AudioLLMs encode structured and interpretable features, enhancing model transparency and control. The framework successfully identifies meaningful audio concepts and enables feature steering.
AI.conclusion: This work establishes a foundation for trustworthy AudioLLM deployment in high-stakes domains and enables future extensions to larger models, multilingual audio, and fine-grained paralinguistic features.

title: Absorbing Discrete Diffusion for Speech Enhancement
summary: Inspired by recent developments in neural speech coding and diffusion-based language modeling, we tackle speech enhancement by modeling the conditional distribution of clean speech codes given noisy speech codes using absorbing discrete diffusion. The proposed approach, which we call ADDSE, leverages both the expressive latent space of neural audio codecs and the non-autoregressive sampling procedure of diffusion models. To efficiently model the hierarchical structure of residual vector quantization codes, we propose RQDiT, which combines techniques from RQ-Transformer and diffusion Transformers for non-autoregressive modeling. Results show competitive performance in terms of non-intrusive objective metrics on two datasets, especially at low signal-to-noise ratios and with few sampling steps. Code and audio examples are available online.

AI.tldr: ADDSE: Absorbing Discrete Diffusion for Speech Enhancement using neural audio codec latent spaces and diffusion modeling for non-autoregressive speech enhancement.
AI.motivation: Leverage recent advances in neural speech coding and diffusion-based language modeling to tackle speech enhancement by modeling the conditional distribution of clean speech codes given noisy speech codes using absorbing discrete diffusion.
AI.method: Propose ADDSE (Absorbing Discrete Diffusion for Speech Enhancement) that models conditional distribution of clean speech codes using absorbing discrete diffusion. Introduce RQDiT (Residual Quantization Diffusion Transformer) combining RQ-Transformer and diffusion Transformers to efficiently model hierarchical structure of residual vector quantization codes for non-autoregressive modeling.
AI.result: Competitive performance in terms of non-intrusive objective metrics on two datasets, especially at low signal-to-noise ratios and with few sampling steps.
AI.conclusion: The proposed ADDSE approach effectively combines neural audio codec latent spaces with diffusion models for speech enhancement, demonstrating strong performance particularly in challenging low SNR conditions with efficient sampling.

title: mmWave Radar Aware Dual-Conditioned GAN for Speech Reconstruction of Signals With Low SNR
summary: Millimeter-wave (mmWave) radar captures are band-limited and noisy, making for difficult reconstruction of intelligible full-bandwidth speech. In this work, we propose a two-stage speech reconstruction pipeline for mmWave using a Radar-Aware Dual-conditioned Generative Adversarial Network (RAD-GAN), which is capable of performing bandwidth extension on signals with low signal-to-noise ratios (-5 dB to -1 dB), captured through glass walls. We propose an mmWave-tailored Multi-Mel Discriminator (MMD) and a Residual Fusion Gate (RFG) to enhance the generator input to process multiple conditioning channels. The proposed two-stage pipeline involves pretraining the model on synthetically clipped clean speech and finetuning on fused mel spectrograms generated by the RFG. We empirically show that the proposed method, trained on a limited dataset, with no pre-trained modules, and no data augmentations, outperformed state-of-the-art approaches for this specific task. Audio examples of RAD-GAN are available online at https://rad-gan-demo-site.vercel.app/.

AI.tldr: RAD-GAN: A two-stage GAN pipeline for reconstructing intelligible full-bandwidth speech from noisy, band-limited mmWave radar captures, even through glass walls.
AI.motivation: Millimeter-wave radar captures are band-limited and noisy, making speech reconstruction challenging, especially through obstacles like glass walls. Existing methods struggle with low SNR conditions (-5 dB to -1 dB) and limited training data.
AI.method: Two-stage pipeline using Radar-Aware Dual-conditioned GAN (RAD-GAN) with mmWave-tailored Multi-Mel Discriminator (MMD) and Residual Fusion Gate (RFG) for processing multiple conditioning channels. Pretraining on synthetically clipped clean speech followed by finetuning on fused mel spectrograms from RFG.
AI.result: Outperforms state-of-the-art approaches for mmWave speech reconstruction despite limited dataset, no pre-trained modules, and no data augmentations. Works effectively at low SNR (-5 dB to -1 dB) through glass walls.
AI.conclusion: RAD-GAN demonstrates effective speech reconstruction from challenging mmWave radar captures, offering a practical solution for real-world applications with limited training resources and difficult environmental conditions.

title: Relating the Neural Representations of Vocalized, Mimed, and Imagined Speech
summary: We investigated the relationship among neural representations of vocalized, mimed, and imagined speech recorded using publicly available stereotactic EEG recordings. Most prior studies have focused on decoding speech responses within each condition separately. Here, instead, we explore how responses across conditions relate by training linear spectrogram reconstruction models for each condition and evaluate their generalization across conditions. We demonstrate that linear decoders trained on one condition generally transfer successfully to others, implying shared speech representations. This commonality was assessed with stimulus-level discriminability by performing a rank-based analysis demonstrating preservation of stimulus-specific structure in both within- and across-conditions. Finally, we compared linear reconstructions to those from a nonlinear neural network. While both exhibited cross-condition transfer, linear models achieve superior stimulus-level discriminability.

AI.tldr: Linear decoders trained on vocalized, mimed, or imagined speech generalize across conditions, revealing shared neural representations with linear models outperforming nonlinear ones in stimulus discriminability.
AI.motivation: To investigate relationships among neural representations of different speech production modes (vocalized, mimed, imagined) and explore how responses across conditions relate, moving beyond prior studies that focused on decoding within each condition separately.
AI.method: Used publicly available stereotactic EEG recordings to train linear spectrogram reconstruction models for each speech condition (vocalized, mimed, imagined), evaluated their generalization across conditions, performed rank-based analysis for stimulus-level discriminability, and compared linear reconstructions to nonlinear neural network models.
AI.result: Linear decoders trained on one condition successfully transfer to others, indicating shared speech representations across conditions. Both within- and across-condition analyses preserved stimulus-specific structure. While both linear and nonlinear models showed cross-condition transfer, linear models achieved superior stimulus-level discriminability.
AI.conclusion: Neural representations of vocalized, mimed, and imagined speech share common features that enable cross-condition generalization, with linear models providing better stimulus discrimination than nonlinear approaches for this task.

title: Moving Speaker Separation via Parallel Spectral-Spatial Processing
comment: Accepted by IEEE Transactions on Audio, Speech and Language Processing
summary: Multi-channel speech separation in dynamic environments is challenging as time-varying spatial and spectral features evolve at different temporal scales. Existing methods typically employ sequential architectures, forcing a single network stream to simultaneously model both feature types, creating an inherent modeling conflict. In this paper, we propose a dual-branch parallel spectral-spatial (PS2) architecture that separately processes spectral and spatial features through parallel streams. The spectral branch uses a bi-directional long short-term memory (BLSTM)-based frequency module, a Mamba-based temporal module, and a self-attention module to model spectral features. The spatial branch employs bi-directional gated recurrent unit (BGRU) networks to process spatial features that encode the evolving geometric relationships between sources and microphones. Features from both branches are integrated through a cross-attention fusion mechanism that adaptively weights their contributions. Experimental results demonstrate that the PS2 outperforms existing state-of-the-art (SOTA) methods by 1.6-2.2 dB in scale-invariant signal-to-distortion ratio (SI-SDR) for moving speaker scenarios, with robust separation quality under different reverberation times (RT60), noise levels, and source movement speeds. Even with fast source movements, the proposed model maintains SI-SDR improvements of over 13 dB. These improvements are consistently observed across multiple datasets, including WHAMR! and our generated WSJ0-Demand-6ch-Move dataset.

AI.tldr: Proposed PS2 architecture with parallel spectral and spatial branches outperforms SOTA methods by 1.6-2.2 dB SI-SDR for moving speaker separation, maintaining >13 dB improvement even with fast movements.
AI.motivation: Existing multi-channel speech separation methods use sequential architectures that force a single network to simultaneously model both spectral and spatial features, creating modeling conflicts as these features evolve at different temporal scales in dynamic environments.
AI.method: Dual-branch parallel spectral-spatial (PS2) architecture with separate processing streams: spectral branch uses BLSTM-based frequency module, Mamba-based temporal module, and self-attention; spatial branch uses BGRU networks to process spatial features encoding geometric relationships between sources and microphones; features integrated via cross-attention fusion mechanism.
AI.result: Outperforms SOTA methods by 1.6-2.2 dB SI-SDR for moving speaker scenarios; maintains robust separation under different RT60, noise levels, and movement speeds; >13 dB SI-SDR improvement even with fast source movements; consistent improvements across WHAMR! and WSJ0-Demand-6ch-Move datasets.
AI.conclusion: The PS2 architecture effectively addresses the modeling conflict in dynamic speech separation by parallel processing of spectral and spatial features, achieving superior performance and robustness across challenging scenarios with moving sources.

title: A Mission Engineering Framework for Uncrewed Aerial Vehicle Design in GNSS-Denied Environments for Intelligence, Surveillance, and Reconnaissance Mission Sets
comment: 12 pages, 8 figures, submitted to IEEE Systems for publication
summary:
Small, low-size, weight, power, and cost (SWaP-C) uncrewed aerial vehicles (UAVs) are increasingly used for intelligence, surveillance, and reconnaissance (ISR) missions due to their affordability, attritability, and suitability for distributed operations. However, their design poses challenges including limited endurance, constrained payload capacity, and reliance on simple sensing modalities such as fixed-field-of-view, bearing-only cameras. Traditional platform-centric methods cannot capture the coupled performance, cost, and coordination trade-offs that emerge at the system-of-systems level.
  This paper presents a mission engineering framework for early-phase design of low-SWaP-C UAV ISR architectures. The framework integrates design of experiments, multi-objective optimization, and high-fidelity simulation into a closed-loop process linking design variables to estimator-informed performance and mission cost. Candidate architectures are explored via Latin hypercube sampling and refined using a genetic algorithm, with performance evaluated through Monte Carlo trials of a federated Kalman filter benchmarked against the posterior Cramer-Rao lower bound. Validation follows the Validation Square methodology, combining theoretical, empirical, and structural assessments.
  A case study on man-overboard localization in a GNSS-denied maritime environment shows that localization accuracy saturates at sub-meter levels, while higher-cost configurations primarily add redundancy and resilience. The framework thus quantifies mission trade-offs between performance, affordability, and robustness, providing a scalable decision-support tool for contested, resource-constrained ISR missions.

AI.tldr: A mission engineering framework for designing low-SWaP-C UAV ISR architectures that integrates design of experiments, multi-objective optimization, and high-fidelity simulation to quantify trade-offs between performance, cost, and robustness.
AI.motivation: Low-SWaP-C UAVs are increasingly used for ISR missions but face design challenges including limited endurance, constrained payload capacity, and reliance on simple sensing modalities. Traditional platform-centric methods cannot capture the coupled performance, cost, and coordination trade-offs at the system-of-systems level.
AI.method: A mission engineering framework integrating design of experiments, multi-objective optimization, and high-fidelity simulation into a closed-loop process. Candidate architectures are explored via Latin hypercube sampling and refined using a genetic algorithm. Performance is evaluated through Monte Carlo trials of a federated Kalman filter benchmarked against the posterior Cramer-Rao lower bound, with validation following the Validation Square methodology.
AI.result: In a case study on man-overboard localization in GNSS-denied maritime environments, localization accuracy saturates at sub-meter levels, while higher-cost configurations primarily add redundancy and resilience rather than improving accuracy.
AI.conclusion: The framework quantifies mission trade-offs between performance, affordability, and robustness, providing a scalable decision-support tool for contested, resource-constrained ISR missions by enabling early-phase design optimization of UAV architectures.

title: Same Words, Different Judgments: Modality Effects on Preference Alignment
summary: Preference-based reinforcement learning (PbRL) is the dominant framework for aligning AI systems to human preferences, but its application to speech remains underexplored. We present a controlled cross-modal study of human and synthetic preference annotations, comparing text and audio evaluations of identical semantic content across 100 prompts. Audio preferences prove as reliable as text, with inter-rater agreement reaching good levels (ICC(2,k) $\approx$ .80) at $\sim$9 raters -- the first ICC-based reliability characterization in the preference annotation literature for either modality. However, modality reshapes how people judge: audio raters exhibit narrower decision thresholds, reduced length bias, and more user-oriented evaluation criteria, with near-chance cross-modality agreement. Synthetic ratings further align with human judgments and predict inter-rater agreement, supporting their use both for triaging ambiguous pairs and as full replacements for human annotations.

AI.tldr: Audio preferences are as reliable as text for RLHF, with good inter-rater agreement at ~9 raters, but modality significantly influences judgment criteria and cross-modality agreement is near-chance.
AI.motivation: Preference-based RL is the dominant framework for aligning AI systems to human preferences, but its application to speech/audio remains underexplored compared to text. There's a need to understand how modality affects preference judgments and reliability.
AI.method: Controlled cross-modal study comparing human and synthetic preference annotations for identical semantic content across 100 prompts. Compared text vs audio evaluations, measured inter-rater agreement using ICC(2,k), analyzed decision thresholds, length bias, and evaluation criteria differences between modalities.
AI.result: Audio preferences proved as reliable as text with good inter-rater agreement (ICC(2,k) ≈ .80) at ~9 raters. Audio raters showed narrower decision thresholds, reduced length bias, and more user-oriented criteria. Cross-modality agreement was near-chance. Synthetic ratings aligned with human judgments and predicted inter-rater agreement.
AI.conclusion: Audio preferences are viable for PbRL with comparable reliability to text, but modality fundamentally reshapes judgment criteria. Synthetic annotations can effectively triage ambiguous pairs or replace human annotations entirely, supporting scalable preference learning for speech systems.

title: Deepfake Word Detection by Next-token Prediction using Fine-tuned Whisper
summary: Deepfake speech utterances can be forged by replacing one or more words in a bona fide utterance with semantically different words synthesized by speech generative models. While a dedicated synthetic word detector could be developed, we investigate a cost-effective method that fine-tunes a pre-trained Whisper model to detect synthetic words while transcribing the input utterance via next-token prediction. We further investigate using partially vocoded utterances as the fine-tuning data, thereby reducing the cost of data collection. Our experiments demonstrate that, on in-domain test data, the fine-tuned Whisper yields low synthetic-word detection error rates and transcription error rates. On out-of-domain test data with synthetic words produced by unseen speech generative models, the fine-tuned Whisper remains on par with a dedicated ResNet-based detection model; however, the overall performance degradation calls for strategies to improve its generalization capability.

AI.tldr: Fine-tuning Whisper for synthetic word detection while transcribing, using partially vocoded data to reduce collection costs, achieving good in-domain performance but needing better generalization for unseen models.
AI.motivation: Deepfake speech can be created by replacing words in genuine utterances with synthetically generated words. While dedicated detectors exist, the paper explores a cost-effective approach that leverages existing speech recognition models for simultaneous detection and transcription.
AI.method: Fine-tunes a pre-trained Whisper model to detect synthetic words while performing transcription via next-token prediction. Uses partially vocoded utterances as fine-tuning data to reduce data collection costs. Evaluates both in-domain and out-of-domain scenarios.
AI.result: On in-domain test data, fine-tuned Whisper achieves low synthetic-word detection error rates and transcription error rates. On out-of-domain data with synthetic words from unseen speech generative models, performance matches a dedicated ResNet-based detector but shows overall degradation, indicating generalization challenges.
AI.conclusion: The approach provides a cost-effective method for synthetic word detection during transcription, but performance degradation on unseen models highlights the need for improved generalization strategies in future work.

title: Small HVAC Control Demonstrations in Larger Buildings Often Overestimate Savings
summary: How much energy, money, and emissions can advanced control of heating and cooling equipment save in real buildings? To address this question, researchers sometimes control a small number of thermal zones within a larger multi-zone building, then report savings for the controlled zones only. That approach can overestimate savings by neglecting heat transfer between controlled zones and adjacent zones. This paper mathematically characterizes the overestimation error when the dynamics are linear and the objectives are linear in the thermal load, as usually holds when optimizing energy efficiency, energy costs, or emissions. Overestimation errors can be large even in seemingly innocuous situations. For example, when controlling only interior zones that have no direct thermal contact with the outdoors, all perceived savings are fictitious. This paper provides an alternative estimation method based on the controlled and adjacent zones' temperature measurements. The new method does not require estimating how much energy the building would have used under baseline operations, so it removes the additional measurement and verification challenge of accurate baseline estimation.

AI.tldr: Advanced control of heating/cooling in partial building zones can overestimate savings due to heat transfer with adjacent zones; paper provides mathematical characterization of this error and alternative estimation method using temperature measurements.
AI.motivation: Researchers often report energy savings from advanced HVAC control by only measuring controlled zones in multi-zone buildings, which neglects heat transfer with adjacent zones and leads to overestimation of actual savings.
AI.method: Mathematical characterization of overestimation error for linear dynamics and linear objectives (energy efficiency, costs, emissions). Provides alternative estimation method using temperature measurements from both controlled and adjacent zones without requiring baseline energy usage estimation.
AI.result: Overestimation errors can be substantial, even in seemingly benign scenarios. For interior zones without direct outdoor contact, all perceived savings are fictitious. The new method eliminates the need for baseline estimation, addressing measurement and verification challenges.
AI.conclusion: Partial zone control studies can significantly overestimate building-wide savings; the proposed temperature-based estimation method provides more accurate savings assessment by accounting for inter-zone heat transfer without requiring baseline energy usage data.

title: A Holistic Framework for Robust Bangla ASR and Speaker Diarization with Optimized VAD and CTC Alignment
summary: Despite being one of the most widely spoken languages globally, Bangla remains a low-resource language in the field of Natural Language Processing (NLP). Mainstream Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR) and Speaker Diarization systems for Bangla struggles when processing longform audio exceeding 3060 seconds. This paper presents a robust framework specifically engineered for extended Bangla content by leveraging preexisting models enhanced with novel optimization pipelines for the DL Sprint 4.0 contest. Our approach utilizes Voice Activity Detection (VAD) optimization and Connectionist Temporal Classification (CTC) segmentation via forced word alignment to maintain temporal accuracy and transcription integrity over long durations. Additionally, we employed several finetuning techniques and preprocessed the data using augmentation techniques and noise removal. By bridging the performance gap in complex, multi-speaker environments, this work provides a scalable solution for real-world, longform Bangla speech applications.

AI.tldr: A framework for longform Bangla ASR and speaker diarization using VAD optimization, CTC segmentation, and data augmentation to handle audio over 30-60 seconds.
AI.motivation: Bangla is widely spoken but remains low-resource in NLP, with existing ASR and speaker diarization systems struggling with longform audio exceeding 30-60 seconds, creating a performance gap in complex, multi-speaker environments.
AI.method: Leverages preexisting models enhanced with novel optimization pipelines including Voice Activity Detection (VAD) optimization and Connectionist Temporal Classification (CTC) segmentation via forced word alignment, plus finetuning techniques and data preprocessing with augmentation and noise removal.
AI.result: Developed a robust framework specifically engineered for extended Bangla content that maintains temporal accuracy and transcription integrity over long durations, bridging the performance gap for real-world applications.
AI.conclusion: Provides a scalable solution for longform Bangla speech applications by addressing the challenges of processing extended audio in complex, multi-speaker environments through optimized pipelines and preprocessing techniques.

title: A Directional-Derivative-Constrained Method for Continuously Steerable Differential Beamformers with Uniform Circular Arrays
summary: Differential microphone arrays offer a promising solution for far-field acoustic signal acquisition due to their high spatial directivity and compact array structure. A key challenge lies in designing differential beamformers that are continuously steerable and capable of enhancing target signals arriving from arbitrary directions. This paper studies the design of differential beamformers for circular arrays and proposes a novel framework that incorporates directional derivative constraints. By constraining the first-order derivatives of the beampattern at the desired steering direction to zero and assigning suitable values to higher-order derivatives, the beamformer is ensured to achieve its maximum response in the target direction and provide sufficient beam steering. This approach not only improves steering flexibility but also enables a more intuitive and robust beampattern design. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed method produces continuously steerable beampatterns.

AI.tldr: Proposes a novel differential beamformer design for circular arrays using directional derivative constraints to achieve continuous steering and enhanced target signal acquisition from arbitrary directions.
AI.motivation: Differential microphone arrays offer high spatial directivity and compact structure for far-field acoustic signal acquisition, but designing continuously steerable beamformers for arbitrary target directions remains challenging.
AI.method: Uses directional derivative constraints: constrains first-order derivatives of beampattern at desired steering direction to zero, assigns suitable values to higher-order derivatives to ensure maximum response in target direction and enable beam steering.
AI.result: Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed method produces continuously steerable beampatterns with improved steering flexibility.
AI.conclusion: The directional derivative constraint framework enables more intuitive and robust beampattern design for circular differential microphone arrays, achieving continuous steering capability.

title: HyperKKL: Enabling Non-Autonomous State Estimation through Dynamic Weight Conditioning
comment: 18 pages, 6 figures, Under review in ICLR 2026 AI & PDE Workshop
summary: This paper proposes HyperKKL, a novel learning approach for designing Kazantzis-Kravaris/Luenberger (KKL) observers for non-autonomous nonlinear systems. While KKL observers offer a rigorous theoretical framework by immersing nonlinear dynamics into a stable linear latent space, its practical realization relies on solving Partial Differential Equations (PDE) that are analytically intractable. Current existing learning-based approximations of the KKL observer are mostly designed for autonomous systems, failing to generalize to driven dynamics without expensive retraining or online gradient updates. HyperKKL addresses this by employing a hypernetwork architecture that encodes the exogenous input signal to instantaneously generate the parameters of the KKL observer, effectively learning a family of immersion maps parameterized by the external drive. We rigorously evaluate this approach against a curriculum learning strategy that attempts to generalize from autonomous regimes via training heuristics alone. The novel approach is illustrated on four numerical simulations in benchmark examples including the Duffing, Van der Pol, Lorenz, and Rössler systems.

AI.tldr: HyperKKL introduces a hypernetwork-based learning approach for designing KKL observers for non-autonomous nonlinear systems, overcoming the limitations of existing methods that fail to generalize to driven dynamics without expensive retraining.
AI.motivation: KKL observers provide a theoretical framework for nonlinear system observation but require solving analytically intractable PDEs. Existing learning-based approximations work only for autonomous systems and cannot handle driven dynamics without costly retraining or online updates.
AI.method: HyperKKL employs a hypernetwork architecture that encodes exogenous input signals to instantaneously generate KKL observer parameters, learning a family of immersion maps parameterized by external drives rather than single autonomous mappings.
AI.result: The approach is rigorously evaluated against curriculum learning strategies and demonstrated on four benchmark systems: Duffing, Van der Pol, Lorenz, and Rössler systems, showing effective generalization to non-autonomous regimes.
AI.conclusion: HyperKKL successfully addresses the generalization challenge for KKL observers in non-autonomous nonlinear systems through hypernetwork parameterization, enabling efficient observer design for driven dynamics without expensive retraining.

title: TADA: A Generative Framework for Speech Modeling via Text-Acoustic Dual Alignment
summary: Modern Text-to-Speech (TTS) systems increasingly leverage Large Language Model (LLM) architectures to achieve scalable, high-fidelity, zero-shot generation. However, these systems typically rely on fixed-frame-rate acoustic tokenization, resulting in speech sequences that are significantly longer than, and asynchronous with their corresponding text. Beyond computational inefficiency, this sequence length disparity often triggers hallucinations in TTS and amplifies the modality gap in spoken language modeling (SLM). In this paper, we propose a novel tokenization scheme that establishes one-to-one synchronization between continuous acoustic features and text tokens, enabling unified, single-stream modeling within an LLM. We demonstrate that these synchronous tokens maintain high-fidelity audio reconstruction and can be effectively modeled in a latent space by a large language model with a flow matching head. Moreover, the ability to seamlessly toggle speech modality within the context enables text-only guidance--a technique that blends logits from text-only and text-speech modes to flexibly bridge the gap toward text-only LLM intelligence. Experimental results indicate that our approach achieves performance competitive with state-of-the-art TTS and SLM systems while virtually eliminating content hallucinations and preserving linguistic integrity, all at a significantly reduced inference cost.

AI.tldr: Proposes synchronous acoustic-text tokenization for TTS using LLMs, eliminating hallucinations and reducing inference cost while maintaining quality.
AI.motivation: Current LLM-based TTS systems use fixed-frame-rate acoustic tokenization causing long, asynchronous speech sequences, computational inefficiency, hallucinations, and modality gaps in spoken language modeling.
AI.method: Novel tokenization scheme establishing one-to-one synchronization between acoustic features and text tokens, enabling unified single-stream LLM modeling with flow matching head; includes text-only guidance technique.
AI.result: Achieves competitive performance with SOTA TTS and SLM systems, virtually eliminates content hallucinations, preserves linguistic integrity, and significantly reduces inference cost.
AI.conclusion: Synchronous acoustic-text tokenization enables efficient, hallucination-free TTS within LLM frameworks while bridging modality gaps through unified modeling.

title: Align-Consistency: Improving Non-autoregressive and Semi-supervised ASR with Consistency Regularization
summary: Consistency regularization (CR) improves the robustness and accuracy of Connectionist Temporal Classification (CTC) by ensuring predictions remain stable across input perturbations. In this work, we propose Align-Consistency, an extension of CR designed for Align-Refine -- a non-autoregressive (non-AR) model that performs iterative refinement of frame-level hypotheses. This method leverages the speed of parallel inference while significantly boosting recognition performance. The effectiveness of Align-Consistency is demonstrated in two settings. First, in the fully supervised setting, our results indicate that applying CR to both the base CTC model and the subsequent refinement steps is critical, and the accuracy improvements from non-AR decoding and CR are mutually additive. Second, for semi-supervised ASR, we employ fast non-AR decoding to generate online pseudo-labels on unlabeled data, which are used to further refine the supervised model and lead to substantial gains.

AI.tldr: Align-Consistency extends consistency regularization to Align-Refine non-autoregressive ASR models, improving both supervised and semi-supervised performance through parallel inference and iterative refinement.
AI.motivation: To enhance the robustness and accuracy of non-autoregressive ASR models by applying consistency regularization to iterative refinement models, leveraging parallel inference speed while improving recognition performance.
AI.method: Align-Consistency extends consistency regularization to Align-Refine, a non-autoregressive model performing iterative refinement of frame-level hypotheses. The method applies CR to both base CTC and refinement steps, and uses fast non-AR decoding for semi-supervised pseudo-label generation.
AI.result: CR application to both base CTC and refinement steps is critical; accuracy improvements from non-AR decoding and CR are mutually additive. In semi-supervised ASR, fast non-AR decoding generates online pseudo-labels leading to substantial performance gains.
AI.conclusion: Align-Consistency effectively combines consistency regularization with non-autoregressive iterative refinement, demonstrating significant improvements in both supervised and semi-supervised ASR settings while maintaining parallel inference speed.

title: Toward Wireless Human-Machine Collaboration in the 6G Era
comment: This work has been submitted to the IEEE for possible publication
summary: The next industrial revolution, Industry 5.0, will be driven by advanced technologies that foster human-machine collaboration (HMC). It will leverage human creativity, judgment, and dexterity with the machine's strength, precision, and speed to improve productivity, quality of life, and sustainability. Wireless communications, empowered by the emerging capabilities of sixth-generation (6G) wireless networks, will play a central role in enabling flexible, scalable, and low-cost deployment of geographically distributed HMC systems. In this article, we first introduce the generic architecture and key components of wireless HMC (WHMC). We then present the network topologies of WHMC and highlight impactful applications across various industry sectors. Driven by the prospective applications, we elaborate on new performance metrics that researchers and practitioners may consider during the exploration and implementation of WHMC and discuss new design methodologies. We then summarize the communication requirements and review promising state-of-the-art technologies that can support WHMC. Finally, we present a proof-of-concept case study and identify several open challenges.

AI.tldr: Wireless human-machine collaboration (WHMC) enabled by 6G networks is key for Industry 5.0, requiring new architectures, metrics, and technologies to support flexible, distributed systems.
AI.motivation: Industry 5.0 requires advanced human-machine collaboration (HMC) systems that combine human creativity and judgment with machine capabilities. Wireless communications, particularly through emerging 6G networks, are essential for enabling flexible, scalable, and cost-effective deployment of geographically distributed HMC systems.
AI.method: The paper introduces a generic architecture and key components of wireless HMC (WHMC), presents network topologies, highlights impactful applications across industry sectors, elaborates on new performance metrics, discusses design methodologies, summarizes communication requirements, reviews state-of-the-art technologies, presents a proof-of-concept case study, and identifies open challenges.
AI.result: The paper provides a comprehensive framework for WHMC systems, including architectural components, network topologies, application domains, performance metrics, design methodologies, communication requirements, and promising enabling technologies. It demonstrates the feasibility through a proof-of-concept case study while identifying remaining research challenges.
AI.conclusion: Wireless HMC enabled by 6G networks is crucial for realizing Industry 5.0's vision of enhanced human-machine collaboration. The paper establishes a foundation for future research and implementation by defining key concepts, requirements, and challenges in this emerging field.

title: Make It Hard to Hear, Easy to Learn: Long-Form Bengali ASR and Speaker Diarization via Extreme Augmentation and Perfect Alignment
summary: Although Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR) in Bengali has seen significant progress, processing long-duration audio and performing robust speaker diarization remain critical research gaps. To address the severe scarcity of joint ASR and diarization resources for this language, we introduce Lipi-Ghor-882, a comprehensive 882-hour multi-speaker Bengali dataset. In this paper, detailing our submission to the DL Sprint 4.0 competition, we systematically evaluate various architectures and approaches for long-form Bengali speech. For ASR, we demonstrate that raw data scaling is ineffective; instead, targeted fine-tuning utilizing perfectly aligned annotations paired with synthetic acoustic degradation (noise and reverberation) emerges as the singular most effective approach. Conversely, for speaker diarization, we observed that global open-source state-of-the-art models (such as Diarizen) performed surprisingly poorly on this complex dataset. Extensive model retraining yielded negligible improvements; instead, strategic, heuristic post-processing of baseline model outputs proved to be the primary driver for increasing accuracy. Ultimately, this work outlines a highly optimized dual pipeline achieving a $\sim$0.019 Real-Time Factor (RTF), establishing a practical, empirically backed benchmark for low-resource, long-form speech processing.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Lipi-Ghor-882, an 882-hour multi-speaker Bengali dataset, and presents optimized pipelines for long-form ASR and speaker diarization, finding that targeted fine-tuning with synthetic degradation works best for ASR while heuristic post-processing is key for diarization.
AI.motivation: Addressing critical research gaps in Bengali ASR for long-duration audio and robust speaker diarization, particularly the severe scarcity of joint ASR and diarization resources for this low-resource language.
AI.method: Introduced Lipi-Ghor-882 dataset (882 hours, multi-speaker). For ASR: systematic evaluation of architectures, found targeted fine-tuning with perfectly aligned annotations and synthetic acoustic degradation (noise and reverberation) most effective. For diarization: tested global SOTA models (e.g., Diarizen), found poor performance, then applied extensive retraining and heuristic post-processing of baseline outputs.
AI.result: Raw data scaling ineffective for ASR; targeted fine-tuning with synthetic degradation emerged as singular most effective approach. Global SOTA diarization models performed poorly; strategic heuristic post-processing of baseline outputs was primary driver for accuracy improvements. Achieved highly optimized dual pipeline with ~0.019 Real-Time Factor.
AI.conclusion: Established practical, empirically backed benchmark for low-resource, long-form Bengali speech processing. Demonstrated that for ASR, quality of annotations and targeted augmentation matters more than raw data scaling, while for diarization, heuristic post-processing is crucial when global models underperform on complex datasets.

title: Opacity in Discrete Event Systems: A Perspective and Overview
summary: Opacity has emerged as a central confidentiality notion for information-flow security in discrete event systems (DES), capturing the requirement that an external observer (intruder) should never be able to determine with certainty whether the system is, was, or will be in a secret state. This article provides a concise, newcomer-friendly overview of opacity in DES, emphasizing core definitions and the unifying estimation viewpoint behind major opacity notions,. We summarize representative verification techniques and highlight how different observation models reshape both the problem formulation and algorithmic structure. We then review principal enforcement paradigms, ranging from opacity-enforcing supervisory control to sensor activation/information release optimization and obfuscation/editing mechanisms. Beyond finite automata, we outline how opacity has been studied in richer models such as stochastic systems, timed systems, Petri nets, and continuous/hybrid dynamics, and we briefly survey applications in robotics, location privacy, and information services. Finally, we discuss selected open challenges, including solvability under incomparable information, scalable methods beyond worst-case complexity, and opacity under intelligent or data-driven adversaries.

AI.tldr: A comprehensive survey of opacity in discrete event systems covering definitions, verification techniques, enforcement methods, extensions to richer models, applications, and open challenges.
AI.motivation: Opacity has become a central confidentiality notion for information-flow security in DES, requiring that an external observer should never be able to determine with certainty whether the system is, was, or will be in a secret state. The paper aims to provide a concise, newcomer-friendly overview of opacity concepts and their development.
AI.method: The paper employs a survey methodology, organizing opacity concepts around core definitions and the unifying estimation viewpoint. It systematically reviews verification techniques, enforcement paradigms, extensions to richer models, and applications across various domains.
AI.result: The survey synthesizes major opacity notions (current-state, initial-state, language-based, etc.), verification techniques (observer construction, diagnoser-based approaches), enforcement methods (supervisory control, sensor activation, information release optimization), and extensions to stochastic systems, timed systems, Petri nets, and continuous/hybrid dynamics.
AI.conclusion: Opacity provides a fundamental framework for confidentiality in DES, with well-developed theoretical foundations and diverse applications. Key open challenges include solvability under incomparable information, scalable methods beyond worst-case complexity, and addressing intelligent/data-driven adversaries, pointing to future research directions.

title: SemanticVocoder: Bridging Audio Generation and Audio Understanding via Semantic Latents
summary: Recent audio generation models typically rely on Variational Autoencoders (VAEs) and perform generation within the VAE latent space. Although VAEs excel at compression and reconstruction, their latents inherently encode low-level acoustic details rather than semantically discriminative information, leading to entangled event semantics and complicating the training of generative models. To address these issues, we discard VAE acoustic latents and introduce semantic encoder latents, thereby proposing SemanticVocoder, a generative vocoder that directly synthesizes waveforms from semantic latents. Equipped with SemanticVocoder, our text-to-audio generation model achieves a Frechet Distance of 12.823 and a Frechet Audio Distance of 1.709 on the AudioCaps test set, as the introduced semantic latents exhibit superior discriminability compared to acoustic VAE latents. Beyond improved generation performance, it also serves as a promising attempt towards unifying audio understanding and generation within a shared semantic space. Generated samples are available at https://zeyuxie29.github.io/SemanticVocoder/.

AI.tldr: SemanticVocoder replaces VAE acoustic latents with semantic encoder latents for text-to-audio generation, achieving better discriminability and improved performance metrics.
AI.motivation: VAE latents in audio generation models encode low-level acoustic details rather than semantically discriminative information, leading to entangled event semantics and complicating generative model training.
AI.method: Discard VAE acoustic latents and introduce semantic encoder latents, proposing SemanticVocoder - a generative vocoder that directly synthesizes waveforms from semantic latents.
AI.result: Achieves Frechet Distance of 12.823 and Frechet Audio Distance of 1.709 on AudioCaps test set, with semantic latents showing superior discriminability compared to acoustic VAE latents.
AI.conclusion: SemanticVocoder improves generation performance and represents a promising step toward unifying audio understanding and generation within a shared semantic space.

title: Transformer Actor-Critic for Efficient Freshness-Aware Resource Allocation
comment: \c{opyright} 2026 IEEE. Personal use of this material is permitted. Permission from IEEE must be obtained for all other uses. Accepted for publication in the 2026 IEEE International Conference on Machine Learning for Communication and Networking (ICMLCN)
summary: Emerging applications such as autonomous driving and industrial automation demand ultra-reliable and low-latency communication (URLLC), where maintaining fresh and timely information is critical. A key performance metric in such systems is the age of information (AoI). This paper addresses AoI minimization in a multi-user uplink wireless network using non-orthogonal multiple access (NOMA), where users offload tasks to a base station. The system must handle user heterogeneity in task sizes, AoI thresholds, and penalty sensitivities, while adhering to NOMA constraints on user scheduling. We propose a deep reinforcement learning (DRL) framework based on proximal policy optimization (PPO), enhanced with a Transformer encoder. The attention mechanism allows the agent to focus on critical user states and capture inter-user dependencies, improving policy performance and scalability. Extensive simulations show that our method reduces average AoI compared to baselines. We also analyze the evolution of attention weights during training and observe that the model progressively learns to prioritize high-importance users. Attention maps reveal meaningful structure: early-stage policies exhibit uniform attention, while later stages show focused patterns aligned with user priority and NOMA constraints. These results highlight the promise of attention-driven DRL for intelligent, priority-aware resource allocation in next-generation wireless systems.

AI.tldr: Transformer-enhanced PPO-based DRL framework for AoI minimization in multi-user NOMA uplink networks, using attention mechanisms to prioritize users based on task heterogeneity and NOMA constraints.
AI.motivation: URLLC applications like autonomous driving require fresh information (low AoI) in multi-user wireless networks with heterogeneous users having different task sizes, AoI thresholds, and penalty sensitivities, while operating under NOMA scheduling constraints.
AI.method: Deep reinforcement learning framework using proximal policy optimization (PPO) enhanced with Transformer encoder attention mechanism to focus on critical user states and capture inter-user dependencies for intelligent resource allocation.
AI.result: The proposed method reduces average AoI compared to baselines. Attention weight analysis shows the model learns to prioritize high-importance users, with attention maps evolving from uniform patterns to focused structures aligned with user priority and NOMA constraints.
AI.conclusion: Attention-driven DRL shows promise for intelligent, priority-aware resource allocation in next-generation wireless systems, effectively handling user heterogeneity and NOMA constraints while minimizing AoI.

title: Steady State Covariance Steering via Sparse Intervention
summary: This paper addresses the steady state covariance steering for linear dynamical systems via structural intervention on the system matrix. We formulate the covariance steering problem as the minimization of the Kullback-Leibler (KL) divergence between the steady state and target Gaussian distributions. To solve the problem, we develop a solution method, hereafter referred to as the proximal gradient-based algorithm, of promoting sparsity in the structural intervention by integrating the objective into a proximal gradient framework with L1 regularization. The main contribution of this paper lies in the analytical expression of the KL divergence gradient with respect to the intervention matrix: the gradient is characterized by the solutions to two Lyapunov equations related to the state covariance equation and its adjoint. Numerical simulations demonstrate that the proximal gradient-based algorithm effectively identifies sparse, structurally-constrained interventions to achieve precise covariance steering.

AI.tldr: Proposes proximal gradient algorithm for sparse structural intervention in linear systems to steer steady-state covariance via KL divergence minimization with L1 regularization.
AI.motivation: Addresses covariance steering for linear dynamical systems through structural intervention on system matrix, aiming to achieve precise control of steady-state distribution while promoting sparsity in interventions.
AI.method: Formulates covariance steering as KL divergence minimization between steady-state and target Gaussian distributions, develops proximal gradient algorithm with L1 regularization for sparse structural intervention, derives analytical gradient expression involving solutions to two Lyapunov equations.
AI.result: Numerical simulations demonstrate algorithm effectively identifies sparse, structurally-constrained interventions for precise covariance steering.
AI.conclusion: Proposed proximal gradient-based algorithm successfully solves covariance steering problem with sparse structural interventions, with main contribution being analytical gradient expression characterized by Lyapunov equation solutions.

title: Integrated Flight and Propulsion Control for Fixed-Wing UAVs via Thrust and Disturbance Compensation
summary: This paper investigates the position-tracking control problem for fixed-wing unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) equipped with a turbojet engine via an integrated flight and propulsion control scheme. To this end, a hierarchical control framework with thrust and disturbance compensation is proposed. In particular, we first propose a perturbed fixed-wing UAV model with turbojet engine dynamics, accounting for both unmodeled dynamics and external disturbances. Second, a versatile extended observer is designed to handle both unmeasurable thrust dynamics and external disturbances. Third, a hierarchical control framework is implemented using three observer-based controllers to guarantee position-tracking performance. With the proposed control strategy, we prove that the closed-loop system asymptotically converges to the desired trajectory. Finally, a comparative simulation is performed to illustrate the proposed control strategy.

AI.tldr: Hierarchical control framework with thrust/disturbance compensation for turbojet-powered fixed-wing UAV position tracking, using extended observer and observer-based controllers to achieve asymptotic convergence.
AI.motivation: Address position-tracking control for turbojet-powered fixed-wing UAVs by integrating flight and propulsion control, handling unmodeled dynamics, unmeasurable thrust dynamics, and external disturbances.
AI.method: 1) Develop perturbed UAV model with turbojet engine dynamics; 2) Design versatile extended observer for thrust dynamics and disturbances; 3) Implement hierarchical control with three observer-based controllers; 4) Prove asymptotic convergence; 5) Validate via comparative simulation.
AI.result: Proposed control strategy ensures closed-loop system asymptotically converges to desired trajectory, validated through comparative simulation demonstrating effectiveness.
AI.conclusion: Integrated flight/propulsion hierarchical control with thrust/disturbance compensation effectively solves position-tracking for turbojet UAVs, handling uncertainties and disturbances while guaranteeing asymptotic convergence.

title: Signal Temporal Logic Verification and Synthesis Using Deep Reachability Analysis and Layered Control Architecture
summary: We propose a signal temporal logic (STL)-based framework that rigorously verifies the feasibility of a mission described in STL and synthesizes control to safely execute it. The proposed framework ensures safe and reliable operation through two phases. First, the proposed framework assesses the feasibility of STL by computing a backward reachable tube (BRT), which captures all states that can satisfy the given STL, regardless of the initial state. The proposed framework accommodates the multiple reach-avoid (MRA) problem to address more general STL specifications and leverages a deep neural network to alleviate the computation burden for reachability analysis, reducing the computation time by about 1000 times compared to a baseline method. We further propose a layered planning and control architecture that combines mixed-integer linear programming (MILP) for global planning with model predictive control (MPC) as a local controller for the verified STL. Consequently, the proposed framework can robustly handle unexpected behavior of obstacles that are not described in the environment information or STL, thereby providing reliable mission performance. Our numerical simulations demonstrate that the proposed framework can successfully compute BRT for a given STL and perform the mission.

AI.tldr: STL-based framework for mission verification and control synthesis using backward reachable tubes and layered planning with MILP/MPC for robust execution.
AI.motivation: Need for rigorous verification of mission feasibility described in Signal Temporal Logic (STL) and synthesis of control strategies to safely execute missions while handling unexpected obstacle behavior not captured in environment information.
AI.method: Two-phase framework: 1) STL feasibility assessment via backward reachable tube (BRT) computation using deep neural networks to accelerate reachability analysis (1000x speedup), accommodating multiple reach-avoid problems; 2) Layered planning/control combining MILP for global planning with MPC as local controller for verified STL.
AI.result: Framework successfully computes BRT for given STL specifications and performs missions in numerical simulations, robustly handling unexpected obstacle behavior not described in environment information or STL.
AI.conclusion: Proposed STL-based framework provides rigorous mission verification, efficient control synthesis, and reliable execution with robustness to unexpected environmental changes through BRT computation and layered MILP-MPC architecture.

title: Millimeter-Wave RIS: Hardware Design and System-Level Considerations
summary: Reconfigurable intelligent surfaces have emerged as a promising hardware platform for shaping wireless propagation environments at millimeter-wave (mm-Wave) frequencies and beyond. While many existing studies emphasize channel modeling and signal processing, practical RIS deployment is fundamentally governed by hardware design choices and their system-level implications. This paper presents a hardware-centric overview of recent mm-Wave RIS developments, covering wideband realizations, high-resolution phase-quantized designs, fully printed low-cost implementations, optically transparent surfaces, RIS-on-chip solutions, and emerging three-dimensional architectures. Key challenges including mutual coupling, calibration, multi-RIS interaction, and frequency-dependent phase control are discussed to bridge hardware realization with system-level optimization. This overview provides practical design insights and aims to guide future RIS research toward scalable, efficient, and practically deployable intelligent surface architectures.

AI.tldr: Hardware-centric overview of mm-Wave reconfigurable intelligent surfaces covering wideband designs, high-resolution phase quantization, low-cost implementations, transparent surfaces, RIS-on-chip solutions, and 3D architectures, with discussion of key hardware challenges and system-level implications.
AI.motivation: While existing RIS research focuses on channel modeling and signal processing, practical deployment is fundamentally governed by hardware design choices and their system-level implications. There's a need to bridge hardware realization with system-level optimization for scalable, efficient, and practically deployable RIS architectures.
AI.method: Presents a comprehensive hardware-centric overview of recent mm-Wave RIS developments, analyzing various design approaches including wideband realizations, high-resolution phase-quantized designs, fully printed low-cost implementations, optically transparent surfaces, RIS-on-chip solutions, and emerging three-dimensional architectures.
AI.result: Identifies key hardware challenges including mutual coupling, calibration requirements, multi-RIS interaction complexities, and frequency-dependent phase control issues that must be addressed for practical deployment. Provides design insights that connect hardware implementation with system-level performance.
AI.conclusion: The overview provides practical design guidance and aims to steer future RIS research toward developing scalable, efficient, and practically deployable intelligent surface architectures by addressing hardware-realization challenges and their system-level implications.
